title: allow rootless podman to volume mount devices without noexec
body:
### Issue Description

Originally commented [here](https://github.com/opencontainers/runtime-tools/issues/759#issuecomment-1846913248). The original issue was `runtime-tools` specific but podman seems to use it's own [way for mounting devices](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/611ba2ff316f09ff49dee4bc354f00b9052dc237/pkg/specgen/generate/config_linux.go#L134-L149)

I'm using the latest `podman` from the Kubic repo:
```
$ podman version
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.6.2
API Version:  4.6.2
Go Version:   go1.18.1
Built:        Thu Jan  1 00:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `$ podman run -it --rm --device /dev/sgx_enclave  --group-add keep-groups --workdir /opt/intel/sgx-sample-app   --entrypoint /bin/bash localhost/intel/sgx-sdk-demo:devel`
2. `# mount|grep sgx_enclave`
`devtmpfs on /dev/sgx_enclave type devtmpfs (rw,nosuid,noexec,relatime,size=1997876k,nr_inodes=499469,mode=755)`


### Describe the results you received

Cannot create SGX enclaves.

### Describe the results you expected

to run SGX enclaves, devfs cannot be `noexec`. Can this be made configurable similar to volumes `OPTIONS`?

comments.body:
This patch fixes my error:
```
diff --git a/pkg/specgen/generate/config_linux.go b/pkg/specgen/generate/config_linux.go
index 965072a0a..48ea149e8 100644
--- a/pkg/specgen/generate/config_linux.go
+++ b/pkg/specgen/generate/config_linux.go
@@ -158,7 +158,7 @@ func addDevice(g *generate.Generator, device string) error {
                        Destination: dst,
                        Type:        define.TypeBind,
                        Source:      src,
-                       Options:     []string{"slave", "nosuid", "noexec", perm, "rbind"},
+                       Options:     []string{"slave", "nosuid", perm, "rbind"},
                }
                g.Config.Mounts = append(g.Config.Mounts, devMnt)
                return nil
```

since you are running as rootless, you could just bind mount the device, as anyway that is what is done internally since an unprivileged user cannot use `mknod`:

```
$ podman run -it --rm -v /dev/sgx_enclave:/dev/sgx_enclave  --group-add keep-groups fedora mount|grep sgx_enclave
devtmpfs on /dev/sgx_enclave type devtmpfs (rw,nosuid,seclabel,size=4096k,nr_inodes=4053248,mode=755,inode64)
```

would that work for you?
I think it does but I guess I'm curious to understand what is happening. What's the difference between `--device` and `-v` then? How are the device cgroups handled with rootless?

device cgroups are not handled with rootless since an unprivileged user cannot install an ebpf filter.

with `--device` you've the equivalent of `-v /dev/sgx_enclave:/dev/sgx_enclave:noexec`

> device cgroups are not handled with rootless since an unprivileged user cannot install an ebpf filter.

does it need some precondition from, e.g., systemd (`DeviceAllow`?) to get the access granted? I'm not able to see anything in my default Ubuntu 22.04 setup for `char-misc rw`.
 
> with `--device` you've the equivalent of `-v /dev/sgx_enclave:/dev/sgx_enclave:noexec`

:+1

> does it need some precondition from, e.g., systemd (`DeviceAllow`?) to get the access granted? I'm not able to see anything in my default Ubuntu 22.04 setup for `char-misc rw`.

it will simply use the same configuration as its parent cgroup.  The limitation is not in user space so systemd cannot help here.  The kernel refuses to install the filter unless you are root in the initial user namespace.

> > does it need some precondition from, e.g., systemd (`DeviceAllow`?) to get the access granted? I'm not able to see anything in my default Ubuntu 22.04 setup for `char-misc rw`.
> 
> it will simply use the same configuration as its parent cgroup. The limitation is not in user space so systemd cannot help here. The kernel refuses to install the filter unless you are root in the initial user namespace.

The default for a user session (`user.slice`) on Ubuntu 22.04 seems to be `DevicePolicy=auto` which allows all devices to be bind mounted. I wanted to test how to control/configure this per device access and that seems easy with a user ID specific config snippet, e.g,

```
[Slice]
DevicePolicy=strict
DeviceAllow=<foobar>
...
# uncommenting the line adds /dev/sgx_enclave access
# DeviceAllow=/dev/char/10:124 rw
```

(a bit offtopic but helped me to learn how this works)

I think I can close this, thanks @giuseppe!
